hook: A bar shaped like a giant bird's nest atop a 1970s skyscraper
facts: Occupies the 32nd floor of the 144-metre Tour Bretagne
Designed by Nantes-born artist Jean Jullien in 2012
Centrepiece is a huge white half-stork half-heron whose body is the bar
Lift access costs one euro; offers a 360-degree panorama
w: On the 32nd floor of a 1970s office tower, a graphic artist hung a giant white bird, half-stork half-heron, watching over Nantes. Its body doubles as a bar; the eggs are stools and tables. One euro buys the lift and a 360-degree city. Cocktails in a nest, 144 metres up.

hook: A banana-ripening shed turned raw concrete contemporary art hall
facts: Built around 1949-50 to ripen bananas from Guinea and the Antilles
Fell into disuse in the 1970s
Reopened as exhibition space for the Estuaire biennial in 2007
Named HAB Galerie in 2011, run by Le Voyage a Nantes
w: Built in 1949 to ripen bananas shipped from Guinea and the Antilles, this riverside shed kept its name long after the fruit stopped coming. Disused by the 1970s, it reopened in 2007 as a raw concrete art hall. Steel, exotic cargo, contemporary installations. The smell of ripening, long gone.

hook: Natural history museum hiding a tanned human skin among the skeletons
facts: Holds a piece of tanned human skin dating to the Revolution era
Legend links it to a soldier who willed his hide to make a drum
Founded in the early 19th century; large comparative anatomy collection
Sits on rue Voltaire near Place Graslin
legend: They say a Revolution-era soldier bequeathed his skin to be tanned into a drum, but it proved too thin for the job
w: Among the skeletons and stuffed beasts sits a glass case few expect: a tanned human skin. Legend ties it to a Revolution-era soldier who, they say, willed his hide to be made into a drum. Too thin, the tannery found. So Nantes keeps it on a shelf. Natural history at its most unnatural.

hook: An 1865 fountain where stone nymphs personify the city's rivers
facts: Inaugurated 1865, designed by city architect Henri-Theodore Driollet
Crowned by an allegory of Nantes above river-nymphs
Nymphs represent the Loire, Erdre, Sevre, Cher and Loiret
Lower basin features genii riding dolphins, symbols of trade
w: Three granite basins climb toward a crowned woman: Nantes, flanked by river-nymphs pouring water from urns, the Loire, Erdre, Sevre, Cher, Loiret. Inaugurated 1865, sculpted by Driollet, it ties the city to its waters. Genii ride dolphins below. A whole watershed, frozen mid-pour.

hook: Church whose belfry was salvaged from a demolished medieval fortress
facts: Parish church of the Bouffay quarter since 1138
Belfry once topped the Bouffay fortress, demolished in 1848
Lantern is ringed by lead angels blowing trumpets
Houses la Bouffay, a bell cast 1663 weighing over eight tonnes
w: The belfry crowning this Renaissance church once topped the Bouffay fortress, demolished in 1848; the city salvaged its clock and hung it here. Look up to the lantern: lead angels blow trumpets in an allegorical round. Below swings la Bouffay, an eight-tonne bell cast in 1663.

hook: Baroque Oratorian chapel now an exhibition wing of the art museum
facts: Begun 1651 by Oratorian priests forced outside the city walls
Facade modelled on Rome's Gesu and Paris's Saint-Gervais
Bought by the town for one symbolic franc in 1963
Now part of the Musee d'arts de Nantes, hosting temporary shows
w: Counter-Reformation grandeur built 1651 by Oratorian priests pushed outside the old walls. The town bought the deconsecrated chapel for one symbolic franc in 1963. Now it hangs with the Musee d'arts: baroque vaults over contemporary shows. Faith dressed up as a Roman church, repurposed.

hook: Postwar garden over a buried Loire arm, named for a teenage poet
facts: Created after WWII on a filled-in branch of the Loire, the bras de l'Hopital
Named in 1968 for Nantes poet Elisa Mercoeur (1809-1835)
Mercoeur published elegies at sixteen, called the 'Armorican muse'
Sits south of the Chateau des ducs de Bretagne
w: Laid out on a filled-in arm of the Loire after the war bombings, this small garden honours Elisa Mercoeur, the 'Armorican muse', a Nantes poet who published elegies at sixteen and died at twenty-five. Lawns and fountains over a buried river. A child prodigy, quietly remembered.

hook: A timber 'swallow's nest' platform cantilevered over the Loire cliff
facts: Designed by artist Tadashi Kawamata for the Estuaire art route
Cantilevered without railings over the Butte Sainte-Anne cliff
Perched roughly 30 metres above the quays
Frames the Loire, port cranes and former shipyards
w: A swallow's-nest platform cantilevered over the cliff edge of the Butte Sainte-Anne, conceived by Tadashi Kawamata as straight timber lines reaching into the void. Thirty metres up, it frames the Loire, the cranes and the old shipyards. No railing fuss. Just sky, river and a long drop.

hook: A 15th-century manor with a spiral stair coiling around empty air
facts: Built in the 15th century in tuffeau and granite beside the cathedral
Named for the cathedral choir school moved here in 1837
Turret stair coils helically around a void, rare for Nantes
Classified a historic monument since 1910
w: Tucked behind the cathedral, this 15th-century tuffeau manor housed the choir school that lent it the odd name. Climb its turret: the spiral staircase coils around empty air, an unusual feat for Nantes. Coats of arms of Brittany above the door. Gothic stone, hidden in plain sight.
